sentences.text: Yep.
--one end of town to the other end.
Right.
From [INAUDIBLE] Strasse, we went to Lange Strasse at [INAUDIBLE].
And we were-- and we were few years living there.
And then this-- we went to my grandmother's house.
We had to live with my grandmother together.
We had to move out again because there was not only laws, even the people were afraid to have Jews as tenants.
That's when we had to [BACKROUND NOISE]  move again.
She lived in Lange Strasse at 81 at the--
The Lange Strasse is Long Street.
It's, you know, translation would be Long Street, went from one end of the town to the other end.
So my grandmother's house was in the center, that way.
Yes because right across the street from my grandmother was the temple, was the synagogue.
And there was an apartment in there.
And that's where the family Katz moved to, this-- the boy, Gunter, I talked about and his family.
They lived-- it was a temple.
And in front of the temple was like a little house.
I guess it used to be for a-- a caretaker or something like that.
And they moved in there.
Not really
I don't remember things like that.
I guess now it was a fact we had to leave.
And I was very happy to move into my grandmother's house to be with my grandmother all the time.
Yeah.
No.
That-- that-- I don't remember that.
I don't think I was upset about it.
Well, my parents felt different about it, naturally.
But on the other hand, my grandmother went on in years already.
So they were happy to be with my grandmother together and could take care of her also.
Yeah.
No.
Till the end, actually, until we were arrested.
But the other thing is that the other few Jews who were still there and had homes, they had to give up their homes.
And there were two Jewish houses, which one was-- one was Silverberg house, which was my grandmother's.
And the other one was right next door, which was the Haineman house, the name of Haineman.
And everybody had to move together in the two houses.
Actually, they were one family all the same, not two family house, but big one family houses.
And we had in my grandmother's house was my grandmother, us, and three other families.
We all had to move together.
So it turned out that some people had a kitchen together.
Try to have little room as a living room private and a bedroom.
And those houses were quite big.
Not the rooms were not that big, but the houses were big.
So in the one family house what my grandmother have, we ended up living there was three, four families.
Yes.
There was a mother with her daughter, Moshaim, and a family, Groontman, and then from the Groontman and Moshaim-- they were somehow related-- the old grandfather, Mr.
Moshaim, he also lived there.
So actually, it was like four different parties, three different parties and us and my grandmother.
It didn't bother me.
I was happy that they were there because the family Groontman happened to have a lot, a lot of books, like a little library.
And I enjoyed that because all of a sudden, I had all the books I wanted to read [INAUDIBLE].
[SIGHS] I would say I was-- '38-- I was 11.
I must have been about 11, 12 years old, 11.
And they-- in one way, they were glad that we didn't have to move again.
And the other way, it-- it was a way of living.
We had to.
Personally, how they personally felt about it, I really don't know.
I really.
Yeah.
I believe you called it a tanner here, people who make from skin, leather.
He was in that type of business.
No, not that I remember him owning the business.
But my grandparents had that type of business also.
But not anymore at the time, you know, during the war-- before the war, or right-- right after, you know, '33 when Hitler came.
So my father was getting skins and would bring it to the tanners.
And they made leather out of it.
That's how I remember.
And-- no.
No.
No, no, no.
That wasn't-- as a matter of fact, I think that was a-- a Jewish businessman-- Jewish business in leather.
And he would sell the skins to them.
But at one time, he had-- but I don't remember he had his own business.
But I don't remember that.
He also was a very sick man because he was wounded in 1914, '18 during the First World War.
And--
He was in the military, yes, like all the German Jews were.
So was he [? is. ?] And he had the, I believe, a gas poisoning of some kind, you know, during the war in France or something.
And he had the open TB from that.
The lungs were affected with it.
So he was a very sick man.
Oh, there was a lot--
There were a lot of restrictions.
There were no children left at that particular time, except that one girl, Moshaim.
And they-- I don't know for what reason or how come, they moved to Hanover.
The others stayed in our house.
But they went to Hanover.
And so I was the only child.
And in that particular time, nobody could leave the town.
And then they started the school, the Jewish school in [?
Herford, ?] and I had to go to the Jewish school.
But also, at the same time, we got the Jewish star already to wear.
So nobody could get out of the town but me.
I had a pass to go on the train or on the bus to go to [?
Herford ?] to school.
So with that, I got acquainted with other people in [?
Herford, ?] and also, with some Christian people there.
And I brought food home and things like that.
Yeah.
A yellow star, Jewish, like a Magen David, like a Jewish star.
And it said, Jude, yellow and black.
On the left side of your jackets or outer garments.
[SIGHS] Yes, that I do remember, because in the train where I went, at that time, at one particular time already, they had French prisoners in that area.
And the French prisoner of wars [BACKROUND NOISE] also was transported on those trains to go to work.
So they saw me, and they tried to talk to me and gave me candy.
I still was a child, don't forget.
And you felt different.
You felt different.
I have to say that.
I never was ashamed to wear it.
I was afraid to wear it at times.
But I never was ashamed.
Yes, it did cause me problems, because at one time, I had-- I don't know if you're familiar with it.
When you had relatives in the States or anywhere out of Germany and you wanted to write to them, you got a little slip at the post office, which you bought, and send it along in your letter.
And they could get American stamps for it, for instance, right?
So I was the only one who could get out of town.
I used to go to the post office in [?
Herford ?] and got those little things.
I had to buy them for my parents.
And I remember very well one time, I was a-- two men came over to me with their badges out.
And they were Gestapo then already.
Gestapo, you know, like little--
--passes that they were from the Secret Service.
German Secret Service.
What-- I don't know what you would call, Gestapo, right?
And I had to open up my books and everything, what I had with me for going to school, because they were thinking that I had things in there.
And so I had--
They were searching you, in other--
Yeah they were searching me then already.
Yeah.
Very frightening, very frightening.
Very frightening.
So ?] this was already before they arrested us.
So I had--
Yes, we're still talking about the period--
Yeah.
Yeah.
Some friends my father had, because he was born in the town, also, so he grew up with all these people.
Even if they didn't show to you outwardly, but they would come at night and brought like food and stuff and would hide it on the side of our building, you know?
So that type of kindness we came across occasionally.
That's ?] [? it. ?]
Yeah, then already because they had the food stamps, like, you know, to get food.
Not stamp, ration cards, right?
And Jews, of course, got less than the others, much less.
So we were lucky that my father had some friends in the town.
In Germany?
Before?
No, no, not really, not really.
I [? would ?] [? lie. ?] No, no.
He didn't work for quite a lot of years.
And then there came a law out that-- I don't remember anymore exactly when it-- it was '37.

Everything was around '36, '37 that every German factory had to employ some Jews, because they wanted the Jewish business people to get out of business, and that they had to work in other factories, et cetera, in German places.
And that time, my father started working also in [?
Herford ?] where I went to school.
But this was before, actually, I went to school there.
And was it '37, '38.
And what happened is-- what was the question again?
You-- you asked me about my father.
If your father was still allowed to--
Yeah.
Now, he worked there, but on a very small salary.
I remember that.
So, you know, this was also already that he had to work there.
And Gunter's father worked in another factory.
And I don't know what he did.
I forgot already, really.
And then they were-- oh, there was one, the Moshaims, the ones who live then in our house, they had a paper factory, a huge big paper factory [BACKROUND NOISE] there.
So a lot of people were employed there.
But they had to-- they took away the factory then already.
So they were very mixed-up times.
I don't know.
I have no idea.
I really don't know.
I know that the factory was [? there ?] in 1938 till the Kristallnacht.
So there's so many different things.
I don't know if they did get paid, or if they didn't get paid, or whatever happened.
And none of that family came back either, so they're the ones who went to Russia.
Yeah?
Yeah, in a way I did, because I was with other children together again.
That was--
--the main part.
Pardon me?
No.
No name, nothing.
It was one classroom, and we were about, maybe-- I don't know.
There are pictures.
There we weren't even 10 children, maybe 10, 12 children, at the most.
And each one had a different age group.
But we had somewhere to go, and somewhere to be, you know?
Yeah, very nice.
He was the cantor of the synagogue, you know?
He was also a teacher.
Levin.
Mr.
Levin.
I don't know his first name.
Yeah, he-- he tried to teach each child, actually, individual, because we all had different knowledges from school because we were different ages.
But he made the best out of.
We learned something, yeah.
No, not to me.
That's the worst for me still today.
I don't even know if we had mathematics at the time.
You know, it wasn't a school where there was a real curriculum or anything like that that you had.
Yes.
They were very worried about it.
And on the other hand, they were happy because I was the only one who could-- could get out of town.
So when they had any kind of connections, I was the-- the little middleman in between already.
It was--
To school?
They closed it up when the transport started to [?
Russia ?] and to [?
Rigga, ?] et cetera.
So it must have been in '41, to '41. '40, to '40, because from '40 to '41, I worked already.
Yeah.
From that particular time?
I don't know what to say.
[LAUGHS] In one way, how I feel about it is I was still with my mother, my father, my grandmother.
We were still a family.
So--
I don't think I ever was scared then as a child, really.
I don't have a good memory of feeling of being scared.
That came much later.
[LAUGHS]
But at this time, you felt--
No, no.
--safe, more or less?
You didn't think--
No.
I didn't feel safe.
But it was a way of not-- not knowing better.
I didn't know really a normal, normal time as a child.
There was always something.
Yes.
We wanted to leave in the worst way.
But we could not leave because my father was sick.
They didn't let anybody out with open TB, number one.
And I had an uncle living in St.
Louis, who was the son of my grandmother we lived with there, who came in 1898 to America.
I think his uncle came and took him along.
He never came back to Europe.
And I remember my grandmother always saying, [INAUDIBLE], her son, would never let her, you know, let down.
She can go move to America.
But he never gave us the-- the visa to go.
You know, they-- you needed a certain amount of money from the States, and et cetera.
We couldn't leave.
Not everybody was the well enough or rich enough [BACKROUND NOISE] to go.
Yes, but--
No.
And I let him know after I came back.
When I came here to America, he came to New York to see me.
And I told him, I said, I couldn't understand that he couldn't do anything for us.
But he should have tried.
Not that my grandmother would have been alive at that time.
She was old already.
But she didn't have to go through that.
You have a son in the States, you could have tried in the worst way, in the early '30s already to get out.
That didn't happen.
He was, I believe, in St.
Louis a baker.
Now, I don't know if he had his own bakery or he worked for somebody or whatever.
And all right, there was a depression and everything.
But there were Jewish organizations then already.
He could have at least tried.
And he didn't
No, no.
No, not at all.
And then I really found out, also, from my cousin, who I met here again, who is the son of my father's sister who came here in 1933-- no, in 1936, '36 yes, in 1936, '37, around that time.
And he was about 15 years old at [BACKROUND NOISE] the time.
Now, he came with the children transport.
And then was in an orphanage here, I believe, in New York for a few weeks or something like that.
And then when it was known that there is an uncle and family in St.
Louis, they got in touch with him, and he went to St.
Louis and stayed with them.
But what I found out that my uncle got paid for him from a Jewish organization to have a foster child.
And there were-- then my cousin also told me that he took in other children to get paid for it.
So I don't know the circumstances, how well off they were or not.
But he could have tried to get my grandmother out.
That's the way I feel.
Benno Silverberg, Benno, B-E-N-N-O, Benno Silverberg.
Guy Stern, and he became a very famous man.
He's a professor in [INAUDIBLE] university, and we are very close.
Not that I can really think of it.
Yeah.
Oh.
What happened is that we were the last Jews left in Vlotho, because the other transports went already, and they took most of the people.
And it was actually my mother, my father, my grandmother, and the family Haineman, which I mentioned before, which were two people over in their 80s.
My grandmother was in her 80s.
They'd all-- Mr.
Moshaim, I was talking about, he was in his 80s.
And my mother, and father, and-- and me, we were the young ones.
So that was the last transport out of Vlotho.
And they came with trucks, and SS, and police, et cetera, and put us on the wagons, on the trucks, and took us to Bielefeld.
And in Bielefeld was the next big town to us, they had the-- what was called the [GERMAN], you know, like where they collect-- a collection of people center.
And then they took us to Theresienstadt.
And that was-- I looked up in the papers.
I thought it was July 1, '42.
It was August 1, '42.
That we arrived in Theresienstadt.
A-- a friend of my father's came from Czechoslovakia for one reason or the other in Czechoslovakia, but he was not Jewish, a non-Jew.
Any he came-- called my-- had left a note for my father-- we didn't even have a telephone at the time or anything-- to come to his office.
He was a dentist in a different town, in [INAUDIBLE] So my father got himself like a little pass that he could go to [INAUDIBLE] for his teeth.
That's the way to get out.
And he told him that in Czechoslovakia, that they emptied out one big town, and it's a ghetto for Jews in the eastern Czechoslovakian area, and also, for a lot of old people from Germany.
And he even told my father at that time, if there would be a way of getting me out to try to get me out of Germany.
But it was too late.
Yes, Dr.
Venkel, Kert Venkel, I believe.
Dr.
Kert Venkel, V-E-N-K-E-L, Dr.
Venkel.
He warned my father, yes.
And we saw him again after the war, my mother and I.
When we came back, we naturally went to him to see him.
Yeah, they came in with trucks.
We know we had-- there was-- physically, of course, physically because we had to get on the trucks.
They told us like a day before to get a certain amount of things we could take along, like clothing, et cetera.
And my grandmother was 81 years old at the time.
And I never forget the first-- the last thing I-- she said when she stepped out of the house.
She said, this is the house where I bore all my children, and now they let me got it.
I have to leave.
You know, just the-- a matter of fact, what I found out about three, four years ago is that that particular house, the Silverberg house was already in the 1600s registered under the name Silverberg.
So the great-great-grandparents must have had it already.
So it's like forced out of everything.
That's it.
Yeah, choice of-- I don't know-- two dresses, and two sweaters, and two skirts, or pants.
I don't remember.
I really don't exactly know what we took along.
We didn't have most of the things we didn't get anyway.
So it didn't matter what you took along, you know?
We went on that truck.
They took us to Bielefeld.
And from all the towns around there was like a collection camp there.
Maybe two or three days.
Not more than that.
They gave us something to eat.
That I remember, soup or bread, whatever.
It started then already.
We weren't surprised at anything at then already.
Didn't know what it was ahead of us, but we are surprised, it wasn't anymore.
No, no, not at that point.
Yeah.
No, he was in Holland already.
He was sent in after the Kristallnacht.
They had family living in Holland, so they, the whole family went to Holland.
And I saw him the first time again in Theresienstadt.
He came in from Holland.
There was a camp Westerbork.
He was [INAUDIBLE].
And he was in Westerbork in Holland.
And then from Westerbork, he came to Theresienstadt.
And--
The-- I-- [SIGHS] the funny part is everybody says it was on the 9th of November.
I remember it on the 10th of November.
So it must have been the two days, the 9th and the 10th.
We were in school.
And there was one of those gym lessons or whatever had sessions there, and we had to leave.
So we went home.
We were on the way home.
And the closer we came to our house, we saw a crowd of people already in front of their house, in front of my house, our house.
And my mother and my grandmother were standing outside.
And that was the time when my father was working in [?
Herford. ?] And the people who, the old people who still lived in their house there, they-- they-- they were outside.
And all what we heard was screaming and the [?
SR, ?] you know, in the ghetto uniforms [? was ?] outside.
And they broke everything in the house.
Not, ghetto.
I said [?
SR ?] uniform.
SA, there was the yellow uniforms they had.
Not the SS, had the black uniforms and the gray ones.
This was the [?
SR, ?] I think, was the storm troopers.
That's them.
The Germans, right.
So they destroyed the whole--
Destroyed the whole house on the inside completely, the furniture, [INAUDIBLE], everything what we had was destroyed.
I mean, from then on, we really lived in broken-up pieces already till they took us.
Right.
That was the last day of school, the German school.
Yes.
They broke-- they didn't put it in fire.
They broke it up.
I think I have pictures in the album there I can show you.
Yeah.
The few stores, which they--
Pardon me?
They arrested-- they arrested all the men, mostly all the men.
Some were sent to Buchenwald and Auschwitz-- Buchenwald and Dachau at the time.
A few came back.
A few did not come back already.
And a younger man, I remember, his name was [?
Antz ?] [?
Swatchauer. ?] He was married to one of the Groontman girls in our house.
And he-- they arrested him, and he went to-- to-- he went to Dachau.
And they were notified that he died there.
Or he got killed in Dachau then already in '38.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's [SIGHS] another story.
OK.
OK.
